_meta.note: Theming + grammar-floor plan for the English A1 deck. Mirrors the Finnish rebalance. wordThemes/textThemes reference data/themes.seed.json ids. floors are FORWARD-LOOKING (the deck is all A1 present-tense today); match substrings test against a sentence's `teaches` tag, sentence takes the HIGHEST matching minLevel, applied push-later only (sentence.level = max(current, floor)). Authored by english-linguist; needs_review until applied + integrity re-run.

notes:
THEMING RATIONALE. Subject pronouns p1-p7 (I/you/he/she/we/they/it) -> basics (the absolute first words). All other function-word pronouns go to grammar: possessives p8/p11, demonstratives p9/p10/p14/p15, question words p12/p13. Copula v1 'be', auxiliary 'have' v2, and all prepositions/conjunctions/articles-helpers/'not'/'very'/'every' -> grammar (the registry's 'Связки и служебные слова' bucket). NOTE on v2 'have': it is themed grammar rather than a content verb because in this deck it functions mainly as the possession/auxiliary workhorse ('I have a dog', 'has got'); if you'd rather it read as a lexical verb, basics is the alt. Numbers f28-f37 -> numbers; days/week/Monday/day -> calendar; clock/hour/time/morning/today/now/o'clock/early/late -> time (kept time and calendar distinct per the registry). Routine verbs (get up/sleep/start/finish) and frequency advs (always/never/sometimes/often) -> routine; 'speak'/'know'/'see'/'like'/'want' are general so 'see'/'speak'/'know' -> basics and the two emotion/preference verbs 'want'/'like' -> feelings alongside happy/nice/fine/good-mood adjectives. Food vs drinks split: solids + meals + eat/cook + food-state adjectives (hungry/thirsty/hot/cold/tasty) -> food; water/coffee/tea/milk -> drinks; 'drink' verb -> food (kept with eat as the consumption pair; could go drinks). restaurant -> cafe, shop/buy -> shopping. Room furniture (room/door/window/table/bed) -> furniture; house/home/garden/dog/cat/live -> home. People nouns (man/woman/boy/girl/friend) + old/young -> people; school nouns (student/teacher/book/school) + study -> school; work/work-verb -> work. Greetings interjections -> greetings; yes/no -> basics. 'this is' demonstrative + possessive sentences derive their theme from words, so no sentence theming needed.

LEVEL MOVES: none. All 151 words sit at a defensible A1 level across the 4 bands; nothing is clearly mis-leveled.

FLOORS (all FORWARD-LOOKING; every one is a NO-OP against the current 46 sentences, which are pure A1 present-tense). I reviewed all current `teaches` tags -- 'to be', 'article', 'do-support', '3rd-person', 'there is', 'telling time'/'at + time', 'adverb of frequency', 'plural after number', 'this is'/'these', 'uncountable', 'be-inversion', 'imperative'. ALL are core A1 and correctly placed within levels 1-4; I deliberately set NO floor that would touch them (e.g. no floor on 'telling time' or 'there is'), so the current deck is untouched. The floors guard grammar English may add later. SUBSTRING-COLLISION CARE: (1) 'perfect' is a substring of nothing problematic here, but note 'present perfect' would ALSO match a bare 'perfect' key -- I use 'present perfect' AND 'perfect' both pointing at minLevel 8 so either tagging style lands at perfect's floor, never below. (2) 'past' alone would over-match (e.g. a hypothetical 'past participle' used inside present perfect) -- I avoid a bare 'past' key and use 'past simple'/'past tense'/'past continuous' so it can't false-trigger on perfect-related tags. (3) 'continuous' is shared by 'present continuous' (L6) and 'past continuous' (L7); I list 'past continuous' under the past floor (L7) so it wins via highest-minLevel, and keep bare 'continuous' at L6 as the safe lower bound -- a past-continuous tag matches BOTH and correctly takes L7. (4) 'can ' has a trailing space to avoid matching words like 'cannot'-free tags or 'candy'; same caution on 'must '. (5) 'future' bare could match 'going to future'/'will future' -- all intended L7, so harmless. If you later add a tag like 'present perfect continuous', it matches 'perfect'(8),'continuous'(6),'present perfect'(8) -> resolves to 8, which is correct.
